did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) strategies combined with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to classify dermoscopy images into benign or malignant skin lesions. The models were trained and evaluated on the ISBI 2016 public dataset, achieving accuracies of 88.02% and 87.43%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of two innovative hybrid CNN strategies with an SVM classifier for skin cancer classification. It details specific methodologies, datasets (ISBI 2016), and quantitative results (accuracies of 88.02% and 87.43%), which are characteristic of a research study rather than a review.

title: Skin Cancer Classification using A Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network with SVM Classifier
journal: 2023 Global Conference on Information Technologies and Communications (GCITC)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of automated skin cancer classification by proposing two hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) strategies combined with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier. The models were trained and evaluated on the ISBI 2016 public dataset, which consists of dermoscopy images categorized as benign or malignant. The methodology involves extracting features from pre-trained CNN architectures and feeding them into the SVM for final classification, while also employing image augmentation to handle dataset imbalance. The proposed models achieved accuracies of 88.02% and 87.43%, respectively, outperforming standard CNN models. These results demonstrate that the hybrid approach effectively reduces reliance on physician annotation while enhancing diagnostic performance for skin lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two categories in the binary classification task (benign vs malignant) on the ISBI 2016 dataset. Specifically, 30 images in the test set represented melanoma.

Used as the other category in the binary classification task. The paper lists specific lesion types included in the broader ISIC/ISBI context (AK, BCC, BKL, DF, NV, SCC, VASC) and states that the models categorize pigmented skin lesions as either melanoma or benign. In the test set, 137 images represented benign lesions.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopy images into benign or malignant (melanoma) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'categorize dermoscopy pictures into benign or malignancy lesions' and 'differentiate benign lesions from malevolent tumors'. The models use CNN feature extraction combined with an SVM classifier to predict discrete diagnostic labels (benign vs. malignant) on the ISBI 2016 dataset. Performance is measured using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2016
datasets_used.description: A public dataset created using the ISIC dataset, consisting of dermoscopy images of skin lesions. The study utilized a subset of this dataset where 900 training images had available labels.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid CNN models. The 765 labeled images were augmented (geometric alterations, 90-degree rotation) to create a balanced training set of 1952 images.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the models. The remaining 167 images (15% of the 900 labeled images) were used as the test set.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision metrics on the ISBI 2016 dataset.

ISBI 2016.notes: The paper reports two sets of results. The abstract states accuracies of 88.02% and 87.43% for two innovative hybrid CNN strategies. Section IV.B and Table I provide detailed metrics for hybrid models combining DenseNet-201 with MobileNet or ResNet-50, classified by SVM using different optimizers (Sgdm, Adam, RMSProp). The best accuracy reported in the detailed results is 89.67% for DenseNet-201 and ResNet-50 with SVM using RMSProp. The conclusion section contains a contradictory statement claiming the DenseNet-201 & MobileNet combination had the best accuracy of 89.67%, which conflicts with Table I where that combination achieved 88.65% accuracy.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Support Vector Machine (SVM) Classifier
preprocessing_techniques: Dataset splitting into training (85%) and testing (15%) sets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to training images to prevent biases and overfitting caused by an unbalanced dataset. Specific transforms included vertical and horizontal orientation flipping (mirroring) and 90-degree rotation.
Augmentation was used to create a balanced training set. The original 765 labeled training images were augmented to generate a total of 1952 balanced training images.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) strategies combined with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier outperformed standard CNN models in classifying skin cancer, achieving accuracies of 88.02% and 87.43% as stated in the abstract, and up to 89.67% for specific hybrid configurations in the results section.

The hybrid approach effectively reduces reliance on physician annotation and enhances diagnostic performance for skin lesions by automatically segmenting images and classifying them as benign or malignant.
Among the tested optimizers, RMSProp yielded the best performance for the hybrid models, with the combination of DenseNet-201 and ResNet-50 achieving the highest accuracy of 89.67%.
The study acknowledges that the current model has room for improvement in classifying a greater number of categories beyond the binary benign/malignant distinction.

Future work should evaluate the performance of the suggested hybrid networks using a variety of epochs, batch sizes, classifiers, and optimizers, and potentially integrate preprocessing processes into the input pipeline.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (benign vs. malignant), failing to differentiate among specific benign subtypes (e.g., BCC, BKL, NV) or malignant subtypes, which limits clinical granularity.
The dataset is small (900 labeled images) and derived from the ISBI 2016/ISIC challenge, which may introduce selection bias and limit generalizability to broader clinical populations.
There is no external validation; the model was only evaluated on the same public dataset (ISBI 2016) used for training, raising concerns about overfitting and real-world robustness.

The paper contains contradictory statements regarding performance: the abstract claims accuracies of 88.02% and 87.43%, while the results section claims a best accuracy of 89.67% for DenseNet-201 & ResNet-50, but Table I shows 88.65% for that specific combination, creating ambiguity about the true best-performing model.
Specificity values are notably low (ranging from 24% to 57% in detailed results), indicating a high false positive rate, which is a significant clinical concern for diagnostic tools.

The paper does not report statistical significance tests (p-values) or confidence intervals for the reported accuracy metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical reliability of the improvements over baseline models.

Data leakage risk exists due to the use of image augmentation (rotation, flipping) on the training set without explicit mention of ensuring strict separation of augmented samples from the test set, although standard practice usually prevents this.
The study is a conference paper presented at a regional conference (GCITC), which may have less rigorous peer review compared to top-tier journals or conferences in medical AI.

The methodology relies on pre-trained CNNs and SVM, which may not capture the full complexity of skin lesion features compared to more recent transformer-based or ensemble methods, potentially limiting state-of-the-art performance.
